message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the quality of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) published on Coronavirus Disease-19 (COVID-19) and to investigate the reasons behind compromising the quality, if found.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A systematic literature search was performed in PubMed, Google Scholar, and Cochrane CENTRAL to identify the Randomized Controlled Trails published on Coronavirus Disease-19 between 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    Dec 2019 to 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    Aug 2021. Research articles met with study criteria were included in the study. Assessment of quality of randomized controlled trials was done using modified Jadad scale.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>21,259 records of randomized controlled trials were identified through database searching, out of which 90 randomized controlled trials were included in the study and, 34 (37.8%) were of high-quality, 46 (51.1%) were of moderate quality, and 10 (11.1 %) were of low-quality studies. There were 40 (44.4%), 38 (42.2%), and 12 (13.3%) randomized controlled trials published in the early, middle, and late terms with Jadad score 5.12±1.67, 5.34±1.32, and 5.68±1.50 respectively (P=0.52). When comparing the blinding status, appropriate blinding, and methods to evaluate adverse events in randomized controlled trials with modified Jadad score, a significant difference was observed (P<0.001). A significant moderate positive correlation was found between the impact factor of the journal and the modified Jadad scale score (R2= 0.48, P<0.001).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Findings from our study indicate that accelerated publication of Coronavirus Disease-19 researches along with the fast-track review process has resulted in lowering study quality scores. With the emergence of stronger evidence, Coronavirus Disease-19 clinical studies with lower methodological quality should be revisited.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Impacts on practice</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>There have been numerous sacrifices and tragedies in the clinical response to covid-19. Revising the quality of randomized controlled trials published on COVID-19 as we enter the third wave of the pandemic and beyond, will improve the evidence-based practice of medications for clinical pharmacy services.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>COVID-19 Patients will benefit from evidence-based pharmaceutical care through reduced drug-related problems.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Can We Really Trust the Findings of the COVID-19 Research? Quality Assessment of Randomized Controlled Trials Published on COVID-19
